title: Homemade gianduja
subtitle: Toasted Piedmontese hazelnuts processed with chocolate into a spreadable paste
excerpt: Gianduja is the Italian original behind mass-market hazelnut chocolate spread: roasted hazelnuts blended with milk chocolate and a little icing sugar into a smooth, rich paste. The Piedmontese version uses a much higher proportion of hazelnut than the industrial product, and the result is quite different.
sourceNotes: Gianduja originated in the Piedmont region of Italy in the early nineteenth century, when cocoa was expensive and hazelnuts were locally abundant. The confection is documented in Italian confectionery literature from the 1800s now in the public domain. The name derives from a local carnival character. The industrial version (Nutella) was introduced in 1964; the traditional paste described here predates it considerably.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a sealed jar at room temperature for 3 weeks, or in the fridge for 2 months. Return to room temperature before using; refrigerated gianduja is too firm to spread.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C; hazelnuts only, toasted until the skins crack

glossaryTerms.definition: Traditional Piedmontese gianduja specifies a minimum of 30 per cent hazelnuts by weight relative to the total confection. This is far higher than mass-market hazelnut-chocolate spreads, which may contain as little as 13 per cent. The higher proportion produces a paste with a pronounced roasted nut flavour and a coarser, more textured result than the industrial product. A food processor will produce a paste that is smooth enough to spread but still noticeably grainy; a high-powered blender will get closer to a fully smooth result.

content.text: Gianduja is hazelnut paste blended with chocolate. The

here is higher than any commercial product: 250 g of hazelnuts to 200 g of milk chocolate. The paste produced is intensely nutty, spreadable when warm, and firm when cold. The hazelnuts must be toasted until the skins crack open and the interior is golden throughout; under-toasted hazelnuts produce a pale, bland paste. A food processor works; a high-powered blender produces a smoother result.

Preheat the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional). Spread the hazelnuts on a baking tray and roast for 12 to 15 minutes, shaking the tray once, until the skins have cracked and the nuts smell deeply roasted. Tip onto a clean cloth while hot, fold the cloth over, and rub vigorously to remove most of the skins. It is not necessary to remove every scrap of skin; a few remaining patches are fine. Leave to cool completely.

Process the cooled hazelnuts in a food processor for 5 to 8 minutes, scraping down the sides every minute or so. The nuts will go through a crumbly stage, then a clumped stage, then gradually release their oils and form a smooth paste. Add the icing sugar and salt and process for another minute. Melt the milk chocolate in a heatproof bowl over barely simmering water. Leave to cool for 5 minutes. With the food processor running, pour the melted chocolate into the hazelnut paste in a steady stream. Process for 2 to 3 minutes until fully combined and smooth. If the paste is thicker than you would like, add the sunflower oil a little at a time and blend in. Transfer to a clean jar while still warm and liquid; it will firm as it cools. Leave to set at room temperature for at least 2 hours before using.
